planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 50–65°F; cold-stratify seeds for 2–4 weeks in the refrigerator to improve germination. Transplant to a shaded, moist wall or fence where the climbing stems have support.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade against a wall, fence, or trellis; Caucasian spinach is a remarkable perennial climbing green from the Caucasus mountains and one of the very few shade-tolerant vining vegetables, making it invaluable in north-facing or deeply shaded spots. The young leaves and shoot tips are harvested throughout spring and summer exactly like spinach — steamed, sautéed, or eaten fresh — with a mild, pleasant flavor similar to New Zealand spinach. The plant dies back to the ground in winter and re-emerges reliably from its rootstock in spring, climbing vigorously to 6–12 feet when given support. In zone 9, provide afternoon shade and consistent moisture to prevent summer decline; the plant often goes partially dormant in peak heat and recovers for fall harvests.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring for summer root production, then force roots in fall or winter indoors; in mild climates, sow in late summer for fall root harvest followed by winter forcing. Do not direct sow too early — chicory bolts in the long days of late spring if sown too early.

directions: Sow seeds thinly in rows 12–18 in apart in late spring; thin to 8–10 in spacing when seedlings reach 2–3 in tall. Grow the plants through summer, then in fall dig the roots (which resemble fat parsnips), trim tops to 1 in, and store in a cool, dark, humid place. To force the distinctive tight pale chicons (the white-leaved heads sold in stores), pack roots vertically in a box of moist sand, cover to exclude all light, and keep at 50–60°F for 3–4 weeks — the blanched white chicons emerge from the root tops. The bitter, tender chicons are excellent braised, roasted, or eaten raw. Never allow light to reach the forcing roots or the chicons will turn green and bitter.

planting.note: Sow in mid-summer for a fall harvest; in warm climates like zone 9, sow in August–September for harvest from October through December. The variety requires exposure to cool temperatures (below 50°F at night) to trigger the formation of tight, red-veined heads.

directions: Sow seeds in summer for fall harvest, thinning to 10–12 in apart when seedlings are established; Treviso radicchio forms elongated, torpedo-shaped heads with deep red leaves and contrasting white veins, quite different in form from the round Chioggia-type radicchio. Flavor is intensely bitter before cold exposure, then sweetens significantly after several frosts. Some gardeners cut the plant back to 1 inch above the crown after initial growth, then allow it to resprout for tighter, sweeter heads — a technique called 'forcing.' In zone 9, the best crops are grown from September sowing, harvested November through January. The grilled heads, split lengthwise and drizzled with olive oil, are a classic Italian preparation.

planting.note: Sow in midsummer (late July to August in most climates) for fall and winter harvest; this Italian heirloom requires cool temperatures to develop its distinctive markings and mild flavor, so timing is critical — too-early sowing leads to bolting, too-late means insufficient size before hard freezes.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in summer, thinning to 12–14 inches apart; Castelfranco is sometimes called the 'Flower of the Veneto' for its extraordinarily beautiful, loosely ruffled heads that open like a rose, with creamy white or pale yellow leaves splashed and spotted with deep wine-red markings — no two heads are alike. The flavor is distinctly milder and more buttery than the sharply bitter Chioggia or Treviso types, making it accessible to diners who find radicchio too harsh. Cool autumn temperatures intensify the color development — heads grown in warm weather are primarily green. Use individual leaves as edible cups for salads or hors d'oeuvres; the visual presentation is stunning. In the South, this is an ideal late-fall and winter salad green for gardens in zones 8–9.

planting.note: Sow in midsummer (July in most climates) for a spring harvest; the plants survive winter and produce their distinctive rosette heads in early spring from the overwintered root. In zone 9, sow September–October for harvest in January–March.

directions: Sow seeds in late summer and allow plants to grow through autumn; after hard frosts, the tops die back and the plant enters semi-dormancy before the root drives up a tight, flat rosette of dark green leaves in late winter or early spring — a delicacy treasured in northern Italy as a salad green when little else is available. The name 'grumolo' means 'little cluster' in Italian, describing the compressed button-rosette form. Grumolo verde is exceptionally cold-hardy and virtually maintenance-free through winter. The bitter, nutty flavor is characteristic of chicory; young spring leaves are the most tender and least bitter. In warm-winter climates like zones 8–9, the plants may not go fully dormant but still produce acceptable rosettes in winter.
sources: Italian seed heritage sources (Sezione Ortiva Italiana)
